text: The paper reviews the use of in situ UV-vis-NIR absorption spectroscopy in catalysis research along with other techniques such as identification of catalytic mechanisms and rates of catalysts as well as structural and morphological properties of catalysts. This includes stopped flow experiments as well as laser pulse experiments with additional perturbations such as temperature, pressure, optical stirring and catalytic reactions over enzymatic, heterogeneous and homogeneous as well as photocatalysts. They work on various time scales for different catalytic reactions and catalysts. Femto- and nanosecond resolved data for transient absorption spectroscopy measurements is used to trace the excited states in photocatalysis. UV-vis-NIR absorption spectroscopy for the structural characterization of catalysts is demonstrated by using Cu and Fe exchanged zeolites and also on enzymes. This requires a combination of spectroscopies, like in combining MCD and Resonance Raman spectroscopy. A large number of phenomena have been traced by applying UV-vis-NIR spectroscopy on transition metal catalysts on various supports such as: changes in the oxidation state of the active component, adsorption reactions, support-metal interaction, surface plasmon resonance, band gaps etc. Quantitative measurements of oxidation states, oxygen vacancies and band gaps have also been shown on heterogeneous catalysts and especially on electrocatalysts. Even though UV-vis-NIR is burdened by the fact that it measures broad absorption bands, it can be developed by using highly developed analysis techniques in order to identify coking reactions on acid zeolites. In conclusion, even if the UV-vis-NIR spectroscopy is very useful for the characterization of catalysts as well as their mechanism, it could be extended to other applications.
